1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: You are a senior actuary working with XYZ Company. The CEO has asked you to explain the board of directors in the next board meeting relevance of reinsurance and to recommend the best re-insurance program available with reasons of choice.

i.question: What factors will determine insurer's appetite for offsetting its risk using reinsurance?

Indicative Solution: The size of the insurer
Its experience in the marketplace
Its available free assets
The size of its portfolio (credibility factor)
The degree to which it is felt that the business outcome is predictable within bounds

Financial Uses: Increase Financial Capacity - Allows ceding company to write more policies with higher limits due to increased spread of risk. Allows small companies to compete with larger companies. Stabilize Earnings - Maximum loss per life, Maximum loss in aggregate, Manage mix of business, Increase spread of risk. Spur New Business Growth - Reinsurer expertise can help in designing and pricing new products, Can share the risk with reinsurer. Improve Balance Sheet Position - Reinsurance may help reduce taxes. If ceded allowance is less than actual expenses, then ceding company may realize additional profits. Catastrophic Protection - Controls risk exposure. Reinsurer will act as guarantor if the ceding company becomes insolvent.

Non-Financial Uses: Increase Intellectual Capacity - Reinsurer expertise may help small companies or companies entering new product lines. Value added services provided by reinsurer. Market research/Competitive research. Underwriting/Pricing/Product design support. Joint Ventures - Direct writer is fronting for reinsurer. The ceding company has a main product focus, but offers other products to accommodate sales situations. There is a mutual interest from both the direct writer and the reinsurer and may structure coinsurance or quota share agreement. Acquisitions and mergers through assumption reinsurance.

iii.question: Describe the key considerations a health insurance company should think about in selecting a re-insurer.

Indicative Solution: What are the goals of the reinsurance program?
What is the financial condition of the reinsurer? Do they have the financial capacity to absorb adverse claim experience?
What are the rates and terms being offered? Beware of rates that are too low and may result in high renewal increases or non-renewal of coverage.
What costs are associated with administering reinsurance? The ceding company will incur additional expenses for managing the reinsurance program.
What degree of management involvement is required? Reinsurance expertise should limit the amount of management time needed.
How much profits are lost to the reinsurer? Insurer should determine how much to cede in order to maximize profits and minimize claim volatility.
Flexibility in terms of the agreement
Reinsurer experience, ability, and expertise - It is important for the reinsurer to make a profit because ceding company's results could mirror reinsurer's results.
What reinsurer services are provided? Do they meet the ceding company's needs?
Business relationship - A good working relationship is important, especially when unforeseen circumstances arise.
Does reinsurer perform own underwriting? Reinsurer may outsource underwriting functions.

iv.question: Calculate the effect of the proposed reinsurance on carrier XYZ's income statement and regulatory capital assuming that claims come in as projected. Show detailed workings.

Indicative Solution: Quote #1 (50/50 Quota Share with 10% expense allowance):
Before Reinsurance: Premiums 1000, Claims 800, Profit 200, Regulatory Capital 100
After Reinsurance: Premiums 450, Claims 400, Profit 50, Regulatory Capital 55
Quote #2 (80% of risk on all claims from Rs 25 Lakhs upto Rs 1.00 crores):
Before Reinsurance: Premiums 1000, Claims 800, Profit 200, Regulatory Capital 100
After Reinsurance: Premiums 750, Claims 596, Profit 46, Regulatory Capital 75

v.question: Recommend the best quote to your insurance company with justifications

Indicative Solution: Quote #1: Reduces the absolute profit by 50 lakhs. Does not have any impact on claims volatility as all claims are shared in equal proportion. Significantly improves the return on regulatory capital and improves the solvency margin ratio. Useful for a company looking for capital relief.

Quote #2: Reduces the absolute profit by 46 lakhs. Removes the claims volatility of very large claims. Improves the return on regulatory capital and the solvency margin ratio slightly. Useful for a company looking for relief against volatility in profits.
Both quotes roughly reduces the same absolute profit. Quote #1 improves return on regulatory capital significantly and would be recommended for a company looking for capital relief.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: You are a valuation actuary working for a Health Insurance Company. You are given the following paid claim run-out information. Assume claims are 100% complete after six months.

i.question: List and briefly describe two methods for calculating health claim reserves.

Indicative Solution: Development or lag method—uses history to project future runouts.

Loss Ratio or Projection method—uses loss ratio or other projection to estimate incurred claims. Generally used when other data is limited such as for new policies or for recent months of incurred claims on existing policies.
Average Cost per Claim Method: Uses average claim cost multiplied by number of claims.

ii.question: Calculate completion factors for each of the six monthly lags using the mean factor method. Show detailed workings.

Indicative Solution: Completion factors are calculated based on the development of claims over time. The mean factor method averages the development factors from different accident periods.

iii.question: Calculate a reserve as of 30th Sep based on workings in part (b). Show detailed workings.

Indicative Solution: Reserve = Latest Paid Amount x Completion Factor - Already Paid

iv.question: Describe adjustments that might improve the reliability of the completion factors developed in part (b).

Indicative Solution: Use more recent data. Segregate by line of business. Adjust for large claims. Consider calendar year effects. Use trending to reflect inflation.

v.question: Identify reasons why the development factor approach might misrepresent emerging experience.

Indicative Solution: Changes in claims handling procedures. Changes in coverage or policy terms. Introduction of new products. Catastrophic events. Changes in reinsurance arrangements. Inadequate data for large claims.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: You are the group health pricing actuary of a leading health insurance company and developing an annually renewable inpatient indemnity product which has an element of cost sharing in it.

i.question: On which claims amounts and what is the probability that an employee paying more in cost sharing in one year under the proposed IIP design compared with the current GHP. Show detailed workings.

Indicative Solution: Calculate expected cost sharing under both designs based on claims distribution and cost sharing provisions. Compare probabilities of higher out-of-pocket under each design.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario: You are working as an actuary in an insurance company in India having significant portfolio of Group Health Insurance. Your company has recently acquired a client for Group Health Insurance with 50,000 employees. A person in charge of Group Health operations has suggested to run a pilot project covering various wellness activities for this corporate.

i.question: What do you understand by wellness initiatives in the context of Group Health Insurance?

Indicative Solution: Wellness initiatives are programs designed to improve health and reduce healthcare costs. Examples include health screening, vaccination drives, gym memberships, nutrition counseling, chronic disease management, employee assistance programs.

ii.question: How some of these could be of help to Insurance Companies?

Indicative Solution: Reduce claims by preventing illnesses. Improve customer retention. Build long-term relationship with corporate clients. Provide data on employee health trends. Differentiate from competitors. Reduce severity of claims through early detection.

iii.question: Prepare the analysis as suggested by CFO and give your comments, suggestions and recommendations to be put before the management committee for evaluating the wellness proposal.

Indicative Solution: Cost analysis: One time registration fee = Rs 2,50,000 per annum. Monthly charges = Rs 75,000 x 12 = Rs 9,00,000. Enrolment fees based on employee categories. Coordinator cost = Rs 5 Lac p.a. Nursing staff = Rs 2 Lac x 4 = Rs 8 Lac. Total cost to company.
Benefit analysis: Expected reduction in claims. Value of improved productivity. Employee satisfaction benefits.
ROI calculation: Compare total costs vs expected benefits. Payback period analysis.
Recommendations based on financial viability.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: You are working as an Actuary for an Indian Insurance Company with sizeable portion of Retail and Group Health Insurance Products. The Company is not happy with the existing Claims Management System and wants to replace it.

i.question: Why it is so important to have an appropriate Claims Management System for actuarial department?

Indicative Solution: For accurate claims data and analysis. To calculate IBNR and reserves correctly. For experience analysis and pricing. To monitor claims trends and patterns. For fraud detection. To ensure data quality for actuarial work.

ii.question: What are your expectations from proposed Claims Management System?

Indicative Solution: Real-time data access. Integration with other systems. Flexible reporting. Automated workflows. Audit trail. Scalability. User-friendly interface.

iii.question: What are the various criteria that you will consider in technical evaluation of Claims Management System?

Indicative Solution: Functionality and features. Technology platform. Integration capabilities. Data security. Scalability. User training and support. Cost of implementation and maintenance. Vendor stability and track record. Customization options. Compliance with regulations.

i.question: How Trend in Health Care Cost of insured population is divided in different parts to measure it appropriately?

Indicative Solution: Trend is typically divided into: Frequency trend (change in number of claims), Severity trend (change in average claim amount), Catastrophic trend (large claims). Also separate components for medical inflation, utilization, and mix changes.

ii.question: What are the factors that can influence overall Trend in Health Care Cost of insured population?

Indicative Solution: Medical inflation. Changes in treatment patterns. New medical technologies. Regulatory changes. Demographic changes. Competition. Economic factors. Provider pricing. Disease patterns. Network hospital expansion.

iii.question: Why is it important to measure and monitor trend in Health Care Cost of insured population?

Indicative Solution: To set appropriate premiums. To forecast future costs. To monitor profitability. To identify unusual patterns. To make pricing adjustments. To assess adequacy of reserves.

iv.question: What are the different methods of measuring trends?

Indicative Solution: Pure Premium Method. Loss Ratio Method. Frequency x Severity Method. Per Claim per Year Method. Blend of multiple methods.

v.question: What are the challenges faced in measure and application of trend?

Indicative Solution: Data quality issues. Lag in data availability. Changes in product design. Regulatory impacts. Reinsurance changes. Unexpected catastrophes. Volatility in small books. Selecting appropriate time period.

7.label: Question 7
7.scenario: You are working for a mid-size Insurance Company in India which is dealing in Group Health Insurance. An expat is taking over the responsibility of CEO. You are required to write a detailed note on Group Health Insurance Product in India covering historical background, trends, channels, product structure, pricing, etc.

i.question: Write a detailed note on Group Health Insurance Product in India covering historical background, trends, channels, product structure, pricing, etc. Also provide list of important points that industry should take into account for long term sustainability of this product.

Indicative Solution: Historical Background: Evolution of Group Health Insurance in India, IRDA regulations, Market development.
Trends: Growth in corporate coverage, Emerging products, Distribution channels.
Channels: Direct sales, Brokers, Bancassurance, Online platforms.
Product Structure: Types of group policies, Coverage options, Waiting periods, Sub-limits.
Pricing: Experience rating, Community rating, Factors affecting premium.
Important Points for Sustainability: Data management, Fraud prevention, Proper underwriting, Adequate pricing, Reinsurance, Customer service, Regular product review, Innovation.
